stories.headline: Child drowns at Cherry Creek State Park after floating into deep water without life jacket
stories.summary:
A child died Saturday evening after drowning in the Cherry Creek Reservoir swim beach area, according to Colorado Parks and Wildlife and South Metro Fire Rescue. Witnesses reported that two children were on a float near the swim beach around 4 p.m. when one child walked to shore while the other drifted into deeper water and went under at approximately 4:25 p.m.

A CPW seasonal park ranger and bystanders jumped in to attempt a rescue. South Metro Fire Rescue's dive team, which happened to be conducting water rescue training in another part of the park, responded within minutes and began searching the water. The West Metro Fire dive team and Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office also assisted.

CPW's Marine Evidence Recovery Team recovered the child's body at 10:20 p.m., more than six hours after the incident began. Officials confirmed neither child on the float was wearing a life jacket. CPW spokesperson Kara Van Hoose stressed that all swimmers, regardless of ability, should wear life jackets, noting that heat on shore combined with cold water temperatures can cause the body to go into shock and impair swimming ability.

The identity, gender and age of the child were not released pending notification by the Arapahoe County Coroner. Cherry Creek State Park was scheduled to reopen for normal hours Sunday.

stories.headline: Broncos linebacker Jonathon Cooper, girlfriend arrested on domestic violence charges
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos outside linebacker Jonathon Cooper and his girlfriend were each arrested late Thursday night on misdemeanor domestic violence charges, according to the Denver Post. The circumstances surrounding the altercation were not immediately detailed, and the team said it was gathering more information.

Cooper signed a four-year contract extension with the Broncos in November 2024 and has two seasons remaining on his deal. The arrest comes during the team's OTA period, adding a significant off-field distraction as Denver begins preparations for the 2026 season.

The Broncos have not yet issued a formal statement on the matter beyond acknowledging the arrest and the ongoing information-gathering process. Under NFL personal conduct policy, domestic violence arrests can trigger league-level investigations and potential discipline separate from any criminal proceedings.

stories.headline: Colorado rancher acknowledges employee shot endangered gray wolf; criminal charges possible
stories.summary:
Fourth-generation Colorado rancher Susan Nottingham has acknowledged in a letter to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service that one of her employees shot and killed the matriarch of the King Mountain wolf pack in March. The shooting occurred on March 10 after a ranch hand reported seeing the wolf running toward cows and their calves; he fired two warning shots and then a third, and the wolf's GPS collar transmitted a mortality signal the following day.

Nottingham's letter, submitted April 7, criticized Colorado Parks and Wildlife for denying her ranch a chronic depredation lethal take permit despite three confirmed livestock deaths attributed to wolves in October 2024. She said the ranch, which spans Eagle and Routt counties, ended the previous year 60 calves short — a loss she valued at approximately $180,000. The denial was based in part on a historic trash dump used by her outfitter during hunting season.

Because gray wolves hold endangered species status in Colorado, the case falls under the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service's 10(j) rule governing when wolves may be harmed. If charges are filed, it would be the first Colorado case in which that rule is applied. CPW said its mortality investigation remains ongoing in consultation with USFWS and that no final determination of cause of death has been made.

USFWS had received more than 31,500 public comments by Thursday on how CPW has handled the wolf reintroduction program, ahead of a June 5 comment deadline. Nottingham faces potential legal consequences for her employee's actions, and she said the investigation has already cost her tens of thousands of dollars in legal fees.

stories.headline: Broncos manage salary cap carefully with Bo Nix mega-contract on the horizon
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos entered the week more than $25 million under the NFL salary cap, providing the front office with rare financial flexibility after two seasons burdened by $85 million in dead cap from the Russell Wilson contract. The team recently gave cornerback Pat Surtain II a $5 million raise with a potential $5 million escalator, a move the Denver Post characterized as smart employee management given Surtain's status as one of the league's elite corners and a likely future Hall of Famer.

The cap management strategy becomes more consequential as quarterback Bo Nix approaches the end of his rookie deal, with a potential market-setting extension looming. The Broncos are also working to restore the practice of rolling over significant cap space year to year — a practice that lapsed over the past two seasons when they rolled over less than $1 million each year, compared to the $5–10 million rolled over annually under former GM George Paton.

The front office's use of option bonuses as a contract structuring tool gives the club additional flexibility in managing future commitments. Coach Sean Payton cited the fluctuating salary cap environment as a key reason for giving Surtain a raise without immediately locking him into a new long-term deal, noting the team views him as elite at his position.

stories.headline: Nuggets' chronic injury pattern draws scrutiny as team watches NBA Finals from home
stories.summary:
With the Denver Nuggets watching the NBA Finals from the sidelines, Denver Gazette columnist Mark Kiszla argues the team's persistent injury problems represent a systemic failure that must be addressed by team president Josh Kroenke. Nikola Jokic, Aaron Gordon, Jamal Murray, Peyton Watson and Christian Braun all dealt with significant injuries that hampered the team's playoff push.

Sports medicine researcher Robert Weissfeld, who has spent more than 30 years in Denver studying injury recovery through kinesiology, neurology, acupuncture and chiropractic methods, described the pattern as what he calls muscle PTSD — a condition in which the brain chronically reduces signals to muscles associated with previous injury as a protective mechanism. He noted that Gordon has never required major surgery but is frequently hobbled, a cycle Weissfeld said is both treatable and preventable.

Weissfeld cautioned that the best predictor of future injury is past injury, but rejected the idea that elite athletes are inevitably prone to breakdown. The column contends that Denver's front office must look beyond conventional training approaches if the franchise hopes to build a durable championship contender around Jokic before his window closes.

stories.headline: $9.5M Cherry Hills Village mansion leads Denver metro's top home sales in May
stories.summary:
A Cherry Hills Village estate sold for its full $9.5 million list price in May, topping the Denver metro area's most expensive residential transactions for the month, according to MLS data compiled by the Denver Post. The 10,000-square-foot home, built in 2017 and situated on a secluded one-acre lot, features six bedrooms, seven bathrooms, an elevator connecting three levels, a primary suite with marble fireplace and sauna, and a lower level with a golf simulator, bar and private theater.

The outdoor amenities include a heated saltwater pool, spa, outdoor kitchen, fire pit and a seven-car garage with two car lifts. The property was sold by K5280 LLC to J King Limited.

Among the other top sales, a 10,500-square-foot custom home in the metro area closed for $7.3 million, purchased by orthopedic shoulder surgeon Ryan Carr and dermatologist Michelle Chevalier. A European-inspired estate in the Denver area sold for $6.3 million after listing at $7 million, while a Hilltop property that sold for $4.9 million in 2023 resold for $5.6 million. The May luxury activity underscores continued demand at the high end of the market even as broader Denver home sales have slowed.

stories.headline: 24th annual Denver Chalk Art Festival draws 25,000 attendees to Golden Triangle district
stories.summary:
The 24th annual Denver Chalk Art Festival took place this weekend in the Golden Triangle Creative District near the Denver Art Museum and downtown library, drawing an estimated 25,000 attendees over the two-day event. A total of 237 artists participated, including five featured artists — previous festival winners — along with amateur and youth competitors working in the streets.

Artwork ranged from portraits of animals to recreations of museum pieces and photorealistic compositions. Denver native Olivia McLeod, who won best of show last year and began competing at the festival at age 13, was among the featured artists this year; her entry depicted actress Mia Goth from the 2025 film "Frankenstein."

The festival, which previously was held at Larimer Square, has been anchored in the Golden Triangle for several years. Judging and public voting for categories including best of show, most whimsical, best reproduction of a museum piece and best use of color were scheduled for Sunday, the final day of the event.

stories.headline: Five Colorado hot springs nominated for USA Today's best in the U.S. readers' choice award
stories.summary:
Five Colorado hot springs have been nominated for USA Today 10Best's readers' choice competition for the best hot springs in the country, with voting open through June 29 and winners to be announced July 8. The nominated Colorado locations span the state from the Western Slope to the San Juan Mountains.

The nominees include Avalanche Ranch in Redstone, Durango Hot Springs Resort & Spa, Glenwood Hot Springs Resort — home to the world's largest outdoor mineral hot springs pool — Iron Mountain Hot Springs in Glenwood Springs, and The Springs Resort in Pagosa Springs. Readers may cast one vote per day for their preferred location.

Colorado's concentration of geothermal resources has long made the state a destination for hot springs tourism, and the nominations reflect the variety of experiences available, from rustic mountain soaking pools to large resort-style facilities with dozens of pools and wellness amenities.

stories.headline: Denver Pride Month and 60th Greek Festival headline weekend community events across metro
stories.summary:
Denver's Pride Month celebrations and the 60th annual Greek Festival are among the headline community events taking place across the metro area this weekend. Denver Pride held its first-ever Mutt Strut, a doggy drag show, at Skiptown Denver at York Street Yards on Saturday, June 6, drawing crowds as part of a broader series of Pride Month programming.

The Greek Festival, now in its 60th year, is one of the longest-running cultural festivals in the Denver area and typically features traditional food, music, dance and cultural exhibitions. The convergence of multiple large community events over the weekend reflects the active summer event calendar in the metro area.

stories.headline: Athletics & Beyond to move into 25,000-square-foot Aurora facility on July 1, tripling its space
stories.summary:
Athletics & Beyond, currently located in Denver, is preparing to relocate on July 1 into a new 25,000-square-foot facility in Aurora — more than three times larger than its current space. The move represents a significant expansion for the business as it seeks to accommodate growing demand.

The relocation to Aurora places the organization within the broader Denver metro market while allowing for substantially expanded programming and capacity. Further details on the nature of Athletics & Beyond's services and the specific Aurora location were not elaborated upon in available reporting.

stories.headline: Polis vetoes six more bills, setting personal record with 12 rejections this legislative session
stories.summary:
Colorado Gov. Jared Polis has vetoed six additional bills, bringing his total to 12 vetoes for the current legislative session — a personal record. The spate of rejections marks an unusually assertive use of the governor's veto power and has drawn attention to tensions between the executive and legislative branches, even within the same political party.

Specific bills included in the latest round of vetoes were not detailed in available reporting. Earlier in the session, Polis vetoed a bill that would have allowed Coloradans to sue ICE agents for civil rights violations, and signed into law a measure creating an enterprise to lower homeowners insurance costs through hail-resistant roof incentives, reflecting a mixed but active signing record this session.

The record number of vetoes signals Polis's willingness to push back against legislation passed by the Democratic-controlled General Assembly on a range of policy issues as the session draws to a close.

weather.notable: About 10 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Have you ever been to a waffle house? It's like being at a friend's house, if your friend was really bad at running a restaurant.
